heroDescription: Non-Bank RMBS Lender • A$15.9bn AUM • 63.6% Controlling Shareholder
heroCompanyDescription: Resimac Group Limited (ASX: RMC) is a non-bank residential mortgage lender with A$15.9 billion in assets under management, funded primarily through residential mortgage-backed securitisation (RMBS). Operations span home loans (~83% of AUM), asset finance, and a legacy New Zealand portfolio in run-off. Controlled by Duncan Saville (63.56% via Somers Limited), with new CEO Pete Lirantzis appointed April 2025.

skew.rationale: 60% combined weight on downside scenarios (N2 Structural Narrowing at 40% + N3 Capital Extraction at 20%). Market pricing cyclical recovery at 1.17x P/B, while evidence-weighted fair value is A$1.02. 1H26 improvement is rate-driven, not operational. Organic AUM growth 4% vs 25.3% sector.

verdict.text: Resimac is trading at A$1.17 (1.17× P/B), 12.8% above the evidence-weighted fair value of A$1.02. The market is pricing a cyclical recovery narrative where 1H26's 163% EBIT improvement marks the beginning of multi-period recovery. The evidence assigns higher probability to macro-masked structural erosion where the 1H26 inflection is the cyclical peak, not the recovery start. Organic home loan AUM grew 4% vs 25.3% sector. Management attributes NIM recovery to RBA rate cuts, not operational improvement. Governance concentration (63.56% controlling shareholder chairing the Audit Committee) and ASIC civil penalty proceedings add structural risk. Verdict: WAIT. The August 2026 FY26 result is the definitive discriminator.

featuredRationale: 12.8% above fair value. 1H26 recovery rate-driven. Organic AUM growth 4% vs 25.3% sector. WAIT until August 2026.

paragraphs: Resimac just delivered its best half-year since FY22: EBIT up 163%, operating cash flow swinging A$456M positive, and the market re-rated the stock from below book value to a 17% premium in five trading days. Organic home loan growth was 4% in a sector growing at 25%. Management attributes the margin recovery to RBA rate cuts, not operational improvement. The question is whether the earnings inflection marks the start of a recovery or the peak of a temporary reprieve.

For two years through mid-2024, Resimac traded at 68–77 cents per dollar of book value, a market verdict that the franchise would not earn its cost of capital. Net income had halved from its FY22 peak, the home loan book was contracting, and the CEO resigned in July 2024. Within eighteen months: new CEO appointed, A$1.4bn Westpac auto portfolio acquired, New Zealand exited, A$161M returned to shareholders at nearly five times annual earnings, ASIC civil penalty proceedings commenced, and no finalised strategy disclosed. Then three RBA rate cuts totalling 75 basis points widened net interest margin by 15 basis points, and the 1H26 results drove the share price from A$0.92 to A$1.17 on five times average volume.

The evidence-weighted assessment is approximately A$1.02, roughly 13% below the current price of A$1.17. The gap reflects a disagreement about what is driving the company. The market is pricing a lender passing through a cyclical trough whose earnings will continue recovering. The evidence gives greater weight to a different reading: that the recovery is externally driven by rate cuts, and that underneath it the company is losing competitive ground, growing at one-sixth the sector rate, with no quantified strategy, governed by a 63.56% controlling shareholder who chairs the Audit Committee. The gap is directional but not definitive. Under plausible shifts in a single variable, it narrows to 2–4%.

The FY26 annual result, due August 2026, is the discriminating event. Two numbers matter. Organic home loan AUM growth, measured against PEXA sector data: above 8% and the competitive gap is closing, the assessment rises toward the current price; below 5% and the company is still losing share. Group net interest margin with the RBA on hold at 3.60%: above 150 basis points and the franchise retains margin without rate-cut support; below 145 basis points and the margin recovery was borrowed from the rate cycle.

Before August, the strategy disclosure promised for early 2026 will either provide quantified targets that make a turnaround credible or deliver another round of aspirational language. That distinction reshapes what is possible from here.

identity.overview: Resimac Group Limited (ASX: RMC) is a non-bank residential mortgage lender with a 40-year securitisation track record and A$50bn cumulative RMBS issuance since 1987. Assets under management of A$15.9bn are funded through limited-recourse securitisation trusts. The company is controlled by Duncan Saville (63.56% of issued capital via Somers Limited), who also chairs the Audit Committee. New CEO Pete Lirantzis was appointed April 2025 following executive turnover across CEO, CFO, and CIO roles. 1H26 showed a significant earnings inflection (EBIT +163%) driven primarily by RBA rate cuts improving NIM from 146bps to 161bps, with no top-line growth contribution. ASIC civil penalty proceedings for hardship compliance failures (January 2022 to February 2024) remain unresolved.

hypotheses.title: N1: Cyclical Trough, Durable Franchise
hypotheses.statusText: Priced: Recovery embedded in current P/B
hypotheses.description: 1H26 inflection marks the beginning of multi-period recovery. RBA rate cuts widen NIM; impairment moderates as credit cycle stabilises. Franchise durable with 40-year securitisation track record. ROE above cost of equity when macro cooperates. Three-year path delivers FY28 net income of A$74M and ROE of 15.6%.
requires: RBA does not reverse course (existing cuts permanent)
Credit losses at or below 1H26 levels (annualised A$19M)
supporting: 1H26 inflection: EBIT +163% to A$39.9M, Group NIM +15bps to 161bps, impairment ↓34%
APRA DTI cap (Feb 2026): Redirects prime borrowers from ADIs to non-bank lenders, structurally expanding addressable market
40-year track record: A$50bn cumulative RMBS issuance since 1987; senior RMBS margins improving
Corporate debt fully repaid: Only limited-recourse securitisation debt remains
contradicting: Organic AUM growth 4% vs 25.3% sector (PEXA FY25, independently sourced)
Management attributes recovery to rate cuts, not operational improvement
Active broker count 1,897 still below historical levels; competitive distribution gap
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: N1 is the only scenario where the franchise is intact and the cycle was the entire problem. The 5–6% organic growth assumption is above FY25's 4% but far below the 25% sector rate. Over three to five years, a lender growing at one-quarter of the sector rate becomes progressively less relevant to brokers, less able to support programmatic securitisation at current scale, and less attractive to the RMBS investor base.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: N1 assumes a franchise earning above cost of equity can sustain that performance while losing market share every period. The risk is that the franchise itself is the problem, and the cycle is merely providing a temporary reprieve. If FY26 organic AUM growth stays below 5% with the sector above 15%, N1 loses its most important requirement.

hypotheses.title: N2: Structurally Narrowing, Macro-Masked
hypotheses.description: 1H26 is the cyclical peak, not the recovery start. Competitive erosion masked by rate-driven NIM expansion. FY26 looks identical to N1 initially (EBIT A$69M); divergence emerges FY27 onward as rate tailwind fades. NIM compresses under competitive pressure; organic AUM growth stagnates while competitors grow at 6x the rate. Three-year path delivers FY28 net income of A$13M and ROE of 3.6%.
requires: Competitive dynamics reassert once rate tailwind stabilises
NIM compresses as rate benefit fades under competitive pressure (161bps → 122bps by FY28)
Management fails to deliver strategy addressing scale disadvantage
supporting: Organic AUM grew 4% vs 25.3% sector (PEXA FY25): independently sourced, not management-selected
Pepper Money originations ~A$10.3bn (~2× Resimac's A$5.8bn); A$21.8bn AUM (37% larger)
Cost-to-income deteriorating: 48.8% → 53.6% vs Liberty at 28%; negative operating leverage (Opex +21% vs NII +7%)
Management attributes NIM recovery to RBA rate cuts, not operational improvement. An admission that works against their interests, increasing credibility
Asset finance NIM fell 37bps post-Westpac acquisition; A$25.4M impairment = 112% of group total on 16% of loan assets
contradicting: APRA DTI cap (Feb 2026) could redirect sufficient volume to offset narrowing
NIM expansion of 15bps in 1H26 demonstrates rate sensitivity works in Resimac's favour
1H26 operating cash flow recovered from negative A$251M to positive A$205M
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: FY26 looks like N1. EBIT of A$69M captures residual rate-cut benefit and SG&A normalisation, producing a result that appears to confirm the cyclical recovery thesis. The divergence from N1 begins in FY27. By FY28, underlying ROE of 8.6% is below cost of equity, the same return environment that the market priced at 0.68–0.77× book for two years. The N2 earnings path is designed to be indistinguishable from N1 at the first observation point and divergent at the second.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: The APRA DTI cap. If the volume of redirected borrowers is large enough, even a subscale operator like Resimac could capture sufficient volume to offset the competitive erosion. The DTI cap is a structural demand expansion that N2 acknowledges but underweights. It changes the size of the market rather than the allocation within it.

hypotheses.title: N3: Capital Extraction, Declining Franchise
hypotheses.description: Controlling shareholder's capital allocation is the organising principle. Cumulative dividends exceed cumulative net income by A$60M over three years. Equity eroding from A$365M toward A$290M. Related party lending escalating (A$8M FY24, A$31M FY25, A$25M 1H26). Distributions of A$161M at 4.7x annual earnings concurrent with 4% organic growth suggest extraction over reinvestment. Three-year path sees equity/assets ratio drop to 1.8%.
requires: Related party transactions continue at current escalating volumes
Management does not materially change capital allocation framework
ASIC proceedings result in material penalty (A$20–30M estimated range)
supporting: A$161M returned to shareholders across FY25–1H26 at 4.7× annual earnings
Related party lending escalated: A$8M (FY24) → A$31M (FY25) → A$25M (1H26) to Saville entities
Saville chairs Audit Committee that oversees the related party transactions he initiates; 3 of 5 directors compromised
No return-on-capital metric (ROE, ROIC, ROA) in STI or LTI compensation despite ROE being disclosed as company metric; AUM growth in both creates dilutive acquisition incentive
contradicting: Saville holds 63.56%: bears full economic consequences; 64 cents per dollar lost falls on his bloc
Corporate debt fully repaid; buyback reducing share count rather than offsetting dilution
Distinction between extraction and rational management of low-growth franchise depends on intent assessment
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: Dividends exceed net income in every year, and this is by design. N3 models the dividend as quasi-fixed, set by the controlling shareholder's extraction preference, with the equity base absorbing the residual. The FY28 one-offs (A$25M ASIC settlement, A$22M full goodwill impairment) are not surprises in this world. They are the crystallisation of risks that the capital allocation framework chose not to provision for.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: The alignment argument. Saville holds 63.56% of the equity. Every dollar of lost market capitalisation costs him 64 cents. The strongest counter-argument: the controlling shareholder is not extracting value but rationally managing a company with limited reinvestment opportunities, returning surplus capital that would otherwise earn below cost of equity. The distinction between extraction and rational management of a low-growth franchise is one of intent, and intent cannot be directly observed from financial statements.

hypotheses.title: N4: Strategic Renewal Under New Leadership
hypotheses.description: New CEO's strategy delivers quantified targets with remuneration linkage. Controlling shareholder permits capital retention. Technology and broker investment produce competitive improvement. Three-year path delivers FY28 net income of A$92M and ROE of 19.6%. Requires fundamental shift in capital allocation that contradicts the current shareholder return programme of A$161M at 4.7x earnings.
requires: Controlling shareholder permits capital retention (A$37M+ per annum retained rather than distributed)
Strategy disclosure contains quantified targets (ROE, cost-to-income, organic AUM growth) with remuneration linkage
Organic home loan AUM growth accelerates to 10% (150% acceleration from current 4%)
Technology investment produces measurable competitive improvement (not "agentic AI automation" without benchmarks)
supporting: New CEO appointed April 2025 with mandate for strategic renewal; broad leadership refresh across operations, credit, technology, product, distribution
1H26 underlying ROE of 15.5% demonstrates earnings power when cycle cooperates
APRA DTI cap creates structural opportunity for non-bank lenders to capture redirected flow
contradicting: CEO 10 months into role with no quantified targets disclosed; 72% of forward guidance is directional or vague
Only 1 of ~14 forward statements is testable: Westpac auto portfolio A$12M operating profit (FY26)
A$161M distributed at 4.7× earnings concurrently with strategy development
Controlling shareholder veto: Saville receives 63.56% of every dollar distributed; all operational assumptions contingent on his consent to retain capital
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: N4 is the only scenario where gross profit grows materially (A$165M to A$219M, up 33%) and the equity base expands rather than stagnates or erodes. The J-curve is visible: FY27 EBIT dips as the ASIC settlement hits, then FY28 surges to A$130M as strategic investment produces competitive improvement on a larger book.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: The controlling shareholder veto. Every other condition is within the CEO's operational scope. Capital allocation is not. The decision to retain A$37M+ per annum requires Saville's affirmative consent, and Saville receives 63.56% of every dollar distributed. If Saville concludes that near-term distributions generate more personal value than uncertain franchise appreciation, the reinvestment pathway closes regardless of the strategy's merits. This is a governance risk that sits upstream of every operational assumption.

narrative.theNarrative: The market is pricing Resimac as a cyclical recovery story at 1.17× P/B, implying return on equity exceeding cost of equity on a sustained basis. The 1H26 result was genuinely impressive on the surface: EBIT +163%, NIM +15bps to 161bps, impairment ↓34%, operating cash flow swinging A$456M positive. But the entire recovery is cost-side and margin-side with no top-line growth contribution. Revenue grew just 2.4%. Management itself attributes the improvement to RBA rate cuts, not operational improvement, an admission against interest that strengthens the structural narrowing thesis. Organic home loan AUM grew 4% against 25.3% sector growth (PEXA, independently sourced). The competitive gap is widening: Pepper Money originations are ~2× Resimac's; cost-to-income is deteriorating while Liberty operates at 28%. Governance concentration (63.56% controlling shareholder chairing the Audit Committee), escalating related party transactions (A$8M → A$31M → A$25M), and ASIC civil proceedings add structural risk layers the market may be underpricing.

priceImplication.label: Embedded Assumptions at A$1.17
priceImplication.content: At 1.17× P/B, the price embeds approximately 48% probability on the cyclical recovery scenario (N1) vs the evidence-weighted 27%. The market is implicitly assuming: NIM sustained above 150bps without further rate cuts; organic AUM growth recovering toward sector averages; impairment staying at or below 1H26 annualised levels; ASIC proceedings resolving without material penalty; and controlling shareholder permitting sufficient capital retention for franchise investment. If organic AUM growth stays below 5% in FY26 while the sector grows >15%, the structural narrowing thesis (N2) gains 8–10 percentage points and the evidence-weighted price falls to A$0.88–0.92. If the August FY26 result shows organic growth >8% and NIM >150bps, the recovery thesis strengthens and the price likely tests A$1.23–1.24 resistance.

narrative.evidenceCheck: The strongest evidence domain is statutory filings (1H26 inflection is auditable) combined with independently sourced competitive data (PEXA organic AUM growth gap). Corporate communications are motivated but honest: management explicitly attributes the NIM recovery to rate cuts, not operational improvement. The governance evidence (controlling shareholder structure, related party escalation, compensation gaps) is objective and verifiable from annual reports. The weakest domains are forward guidance credibility (72% directional or vague) and strategic renewal probability (no quantified targets disclosed).
narrative.narrativeStability: LOW: Binary outcome driven by FY26 annual result in August 2026. The next 5 months will confirm whether 1H26 was a cyclical trough or a cyclical peak. High sensitivity to NIM (5bps change = ±A$8–9M NII = ±1.3–1.5ppt ROE). Thin equity buffer (2.4% of assets) amplifies both upside and downside. Strategy disclosure timing is an additional uncertainty.

qualityOfEarnings.inflectionDependency: Three converging factors drive the half-year improvement: NIM widened 15 basis points to 161bps as three RBA rate cuts flowed through the variable-rate book; impairment halved as credit stress moderated and FY25 write-off policy acceleration cleared stale receivables; and SG&A compressed. Revenue grew 2.4%. The entire profitability recovery is cost-side and margin-side, with no contribution from top-line growth. If the rate cycle pauses, impairment re-accelerates, or SG&A reverts, the improvement reverses on a flat revenue base.
qualityOfEarnings.threePhaseArc: The arc divides into three phases. FY22 was the peak: EBIT margin of 26.3% and ROE of 18.4%. FY23 through FY25 was sustained compression: EBIT fell from A$120M to A$44M as impairment expense rose tenfold, SG&A grew faster than revenue, and the home loan book contracted A$1.8bn before partially recovering. 1H26 is the inflection: EBIT margin expanded 460 basis points same-half year-on-year, impairment halved, and operating cash flow swung from negative A$251M to positive A$205M.
qualityOfEarnings.roeFlatterers: The FY25 ROE of 9.5% is almost entirely leverage-driven. EBIT margin contracted and asset turnover fell; the equity multiplier expanded from 36× to 46× because A$1.9bn in securitisation debt was added while equity declined A$51M through dividends exceeding earnings. The 1H26 normalised ROE of 15.5% is partly a denominator effect: equity fell from A$416M to A$365M through A$161M in capital returns. On a stable equity base, the ROE improvement would be materially smaller.
qualityOfEarnings.balanceSheetFragility: The buffer is thin. The A$25.4M asset finance impairment in FY25 alone consumed 6.4% of equity. If the accounting section's stress scenario materialised concurrently with the ASIC contingency, the combined A$35–49M impact would reduce equity by 8.8–12.3%.

evidence.intro: Ten evidence domains assessed across the epistemic hierarchy. Evidence ranked: Audited Statutory > Objective Data > Independent Sources > Consensus > Motivated > Noise.

cards.title: 1. Corporate Communications
cards.finding: 1H26 earnings beat driven by margin, not volume. EBIT +163% to A$39.9M with Group NIM widening 15bps to 161bps. Revenue grew just 2.4%. Management's five-point strategy (broker investment, asset finance reset, technology, product simplification, cost discipline) contains no quantified targets. Of ~14 forward statements, only the Westpac auto portfolio operating profit target (A$12M, FY26) is testable. Management explicitly attributes recovery to RBA rate cuts, a statement against interest that increases credibility but undermines the franchise thesis.
cards.tension: Management's honest attribution of recovery to rate cuts contradicts the market's franchise recovery pricing. The absence of quantified targets 10 months into the CEO's tenure is notable given the breadth of the announced strategy.
cards.source: RMC 1H26 Results Presentation, Feb 2026; FY25 Annual Report

cards.title: 2. Regulatory Filings & Financial Statements
cards.finding: 1H26 inflection is auditable and real, but equity base is thinning. ROE recovered to ~15.5% (annualised), but equity declined from A$416M to A$365M through A$161M in capital returns at 4.7× annual earnings. ECL provisioning shows directional conflict: Loss Given Default reduced 50bps during FY25 while Stage 3 balances rose 87% to A$200M and impairment surged 184%. Alternative reasonable ECL assumptions could produce provisions A$15–25M above reported, equal to 43–72% of statutory NPAT. Key audit matters flagged ECL model rebuild risk and goodwill impairment (market cap below book at two consecutive dates).
cards.tension: The improvement is mathematically real but partially manufactured by denominator effect (equity reduction) and ECL assumption choices that may not survive a prolonged downturn. Goodwill on HLLB CGU (A$21.7M) has negative headroom under stress.
cards.source: Resimac FY25 Annual Report (Deloitte audited); 1H26 Appendix 4D; Key Audit Matters

cards.title: 3. Broker & Consensus Estimates
cards.finding: Limited coverage reflects small-cap status. Sparse broker coverage for a A$463M market cap non-bank lender. Available estimates tend toward the cyclical recovery narrative (N1), anchoring on the 1H26 inflection without fully weighting the organic growth gap or governance structure. Consensus likely underweights the competitive erosion evident in independently sourced PEXA data.
cards.tension: The lack of sell-side coverage means the market may be pricing off limited analytical depth. Small-cap non-bank lenders attract minimal institutional research, creating information asymmetry that benefits the controlling shareholder.

cards.title: 4. Competitor & Industry Data
cards.finding: Organic growth gap is the single most diagnostic data point. Resimac organic home loan AUM grew 4% (FY25) vs non-ADI sector growth of 25.3% (PEXA). Pepper Money originations reached ~A$10.3bn (~2× Resimac's A$5.8bn) with AUM of A$21.8bn (37% larger). Liberty operates at 28% cost-to-income vs Resimac's 53.6%. Non-bank RMBS issuance rose to A$33.9bn (2024) from A$25bn (2023), indicating sector health that Resimac is not capturing proportionally.
cards.tension: The sector is growing rapidly, funding markets are supportive, and competitors are scaling, yet Resimac is growing at one-sixth the rate. This is the strongest evidence for structural narrowing and the hardest data point for the cyclical recovery thesis to explain.
cards.source: PEXA FY25 Mortgage Insights; Pepper Money FY25 Results; Liberty FY25 Results; APRA ADI Statistics

cards.title: 5. Economic & Regulatory Data
cards.finding: RBA rate cuts are the primary driver; APRA DTI cap is untested. Three RBA rate cuts of 25bps each through FY26 directly widened Resimac's NIM by 15bps to 161bps. The APRA DTI cap (effective February 2026) restricts ADI lending to borrowers with debt exceeding 6× income, potentially redirecting flow to non-bank lenders. However, Resimac's specific capture rate from DTI-redirected borrowers is unknown. RMBS senior margins have tightened, supporting funding costs.
cards.tension: Rate cuts help all non-bank lenders equally, not Resimac specifically. The DTI cap is structurally positive for the sector but may disproportionately benefit larger, better-distributed competitors (Pepper, Firstmac) who can capture redirected volume more effectively.
cards.source: RBA Official Cash Rate decisions; APRA DTI cap announcement Feb 2026; RMBS market data

cards.title: 6. Price Action & Market Behaviour
cards.finding: Market belief shifted sharply on 1H26 result. RMC rallied 18.9% in a single session on 5× average volume following the 1H26 result. The stock re-rated from below book value to a 17% premium. Prior to the result, the stock had recovered 55.7% from the July 2024 trough of A$0.79 to the October 2025 high of A$1.23, then consolidated around A$0.92–1.00 before the 1H26 catalyst. The single-day advance suggests the market was underweight the recovery scenario pre-result.
cards.tension: The magnitude of the single-day move (18.9%) suggests the market was caught underweight. But single-session re-ratings on earnings beats frequently overshoot, particularly in small-cap stocks with limited float. The 1.17× P/B pricing may be the market's first take, not its considered view.
cards.source: ASX market data; RMC price and volume history

cards.title: 7. Academic & Structural Research
cards.finding: Controlling shareholder literature flags minority expropriation risk. Academic research on concentrated ownership structures consistently finds that controlling shareholders above 50% can extract private benefits through related party transactions, preferential capital allocation, and information asymmetry. Non-bank lender structures funded through limited-recourse securitisation create additional agency risks as the corporate entity's equity buffer is thin relative to assets managed. RMBS market structure research shows non-bank lenders face pro-cyclical funding risk during market stress.
cards.tension: The academic framework supports heightened scrutiny of Saville's 63.56% control, but concentrated ownership also aligns incentives when the controlling shareholder bears majority economic loss. The empirical evidence suggests both value creation and extraction occur in controlled companies, depending on governance quality.
cards.source: Corporate governance literature; RMBS market structure research

cards.title: 8. Media & Public Commentary
cards.finding: ASIC proceedings generate periodic negative coverage. The ASIC civil penalty proceedings for hardship compliance failures (January 2022 to February 2024) have attracted media attention. Broader non-bank sector coverage tends to focus on the DTI cap opportunity and RMBS market conditions. No material insider activity or unusual corporate actions detected beyond the established capital return programme.
cards.tension: Media coverage amplifies the governance narrative but adds limited analytical value beyond the statutory evidence. The ASIC proceedings are factual; the risk is quantification (no contingent liability recognised, estimated exposure A$20–30M from public precedent).

cards.title: 9. Leadership & Governance Assessment
cards.finding: Governance concentration is structural, not transitional. Duncan Saville (63.56% via Somers Limited) chairs the Audit Committee that oversees related party transactions he initiates. Board composition: 3 compromised directors, 2 independent. Wayne Spanner chairs the Board, Remuneration & Nomination, and Risk committees simultaneously. Executive turnover across CEO, CFO, and CIO in FY25 creates execution risk. No return-on-capital metric (ROE, ROIC, ROA) exists in STI or LTI compensation despite ROE being disclosed as a company metric. AUM growth targets in both STI and LTI create dilutive acquisition incentive.
cards.tension: The governance structure is unusual but not illegal. Saville's majority ownership means he bears 64% of any value destruction, creating natural alignment. The question is whether the capital return programme (A$161M at 4.7x earnings) reflects rational management of a low-growth franchise or minority expropriation through excessive distributions.
cards.source: Resimac FY25 Annual Report; Related Party Disclosures; Remuneration Report

cards.title: 10. Ownership & Capital Flows
cards.finding: Capital allocation reveals priorities. Somers Limited (Saville) holds 63.56% of issued capital. Capital returns of A$161M across FY25–1H26 equate to 4.7× annual earnings, with ~A$102M flowing to Saville's bloc. Equity declined from A$416M to A$365M while operating in a growing market. The company simultaneously distributed capital and referenced "agentic AI automation" as a strategic initiative without quantified investment, timeline, or benchmarks. Related party lending to Saville entities escalated from A$8M (FY24) to A$31M (FY25) to A$25M (1H26).
cards.tension: High payout ratios in a low-growth franchise can be rational (returning excess capital). The question is whether A$161M at 4.7x earnings is excess capital or equity erosion. The concurrent absence of reinvestment in competitive position (organic AUM growing at one-sixth the sector rate) suggests the latter.
cards.source: ASX substantial holder notices; Resimac FY25 Annual Report; Appendix 4D

discriminators.intro: Four data points carry high diagnosticity for discriminating between Resimac's competing hypotheses.

rows.evidence: FY26 organic home loan AUM growth vs PEXA sector data. The single most diagnostic metric. Organic growth above 8% validates the franchise thesis (N1); below 5% while the sector grows above 15% confirms structural narrowing (N2).
Group NIM with RBA on hold at 3.60%. NIM above 150bps with rates stable indicates franchise margin power. NIM below 145bps indicates rate-driven reprieve now fading under competitive pressure.
rows.currentReading: 161bps at 1H26 with 75bps cumulative cuts: directionally ambiguous

rows.evidence: Strategy disclosure with quantified targets and remuneration linkage. Quantified targets with interim milestones activate N4 (Strategic Renewal). Qualitative iteration forecloses N4 and redistributes weight to N2 and N3.
rows.currentReading: 72% of guidance directional or vague; 1 of ~14 statements testable

rows.evidence: Capital return rate vs earnings power (FY26 dividend and buyback programme). Returns exceeding earnings confirm extraction trajectory (N3). Retention shift confirms renewal potential (N4).
rows.currentReading: A$161M distributed at 4.7× FY25 NPAT: extraction pattern

discriminators.nonDiscriminating: Broad ASX financials sector performance • General interest rate expectations • Non-bank sector sentiment indices • RMBS spread movements (affect all non-bank lenders equally and do not specifically discriminate between Resimac's competing hypotheses).

tripwires.intro: Three catalysts that would trigger reassessment of hypothesis weights, with specific thresholds defining positive and negative outcomes.

cards.name: FY26 Annual Result: The Definitive Test

conditions.if: If organic home loan AUM growth >8%, Group NIM >150bps with RBA on hold, and full-year impairment <A$18M
conditions.then: N1 (Cyclical Trough) gains 8–10 percentage points to 35–37%. Evidence-weighted price rises to A$1.14–1.18. Stock likely tests A$1.23–1.24 resistance. Gap closes; alignment with market pricing.

conditions.if: If organic AUM growth <5% with sector >15%, NIM <145bps, or impairment >A$22M
conditions.then: N2 (Structural Narrowing) gains 8–10 percentage points to 48–50%. Evidence-weighted price falls to A$0.88–0.92. Gap widens to 20–25%. Market reprices from 1.17× P/B toward 0.80–0.90×.

cards.source: Source: Resimac ASX FY26 Annual Report and Appendix 4E

cards.name: Strategy Disclosure: Quantified or Qualitative?

conditions.if: If strategy includes quantified targets (ROE, cost-to-income, organic AUM growth) with interim milestones and remuneration linkage
conditions.then: N4 (Strategic Renewal) gains 5–8 percentage points. Evidence-weighted price rises A$0.08–0.12. Provides first tangible evidence of franchise investment.

conditions.if: If strategy is a qualitative iteration with no measurable targets or remuneration linkage
conditions.then: N4 (Strategic Renewal) foreclosed as viable. Weight redistributes proportionally to N2 and N3. Evidence-weighted price falls A$0.03–0.05.

cards.source: Source: Company strategy disclosure; investor presentations

cards.name: ASIC Proceedings Outcome

conditions.if: If settlement or minor penalty (<A$5M) with standard compliance undertakings
conditions.then: Removes contingent A$20–30M liability overhang. Marginal positive for all scenarios. N3 (Capital Extraction) loses ~2 percentage points.

conditions.if: If material penalty (A$20–30M) with ongoing compliance obligations and increased operational costs
conditions.then: Crystallises 58–87% of one year's NPAT. Strengthens N3 (Capital Extraction). Evidence-weighted price falls A$0.05–0.08.

cards.source: Source: ASIC civil penalty proceedings; Federal Court of Australia

couldntAssess: Market share trend data: No time-series competitive positioning analysis available; reliant on single-period PEXA comparison
Broker metrics: No retention, attrition, or concentration risk data disclosed
Westpac auto portfolio: Post-acquisition integration performance and ECL coverage not separately disclosed
Technology capability: "Agentic AI automation" referenced with no quantified investment, timeline, or benchmarks
Organic growth attribution: No disclosure of which products, channels, or geographies drove the 4% growth
ECL sensitivity: No disclosure of impact from scenario weight shifts or LGD reversal to prior assumptions

ECL provisioning relies on discretionary assumptions that could materially affect reported earnings (alternative reasonable assumptions produce provisions A$15–25M above reported, equal to 43–72% of statutory NPAT). The August 2026 FY26 result is the definitive discriminator between competing hypotheses.

riskAsymmetry.summary: The downside scenarios (N2 at A$0.69, N3 at A$0.47) carry 60% combined weight and sit 41–60% below the current price; the upside scenarios (N1 at A$1.41, N4 at A$2.05) carry 40% combined weight and sit 21–75% above it. The expected loss from the downside scenarios exceeds the expected gain from the upside.
riskAsymmetry.marketDisagreement: The evidence and the market disagree most on the probability of cyclical recovery. The market assigns approximately 48% to N1; the evidence assigns 27%. The mirror image: the evidence assigns 40% to N2; the market approximately 27%. This is a single disagreement expressed through two numbers: whether the 1H26 improvement is the beginning of a multi-period recovery or the peak of a rate-driven reprieve. One captures a moment; the other captures a structure.

conclusion.opening: The gap of 12.8% warrants attention but not conviction. It narrows to 2–4% under plausible perturbations and depends on a single disagreement about whether competitive erosion or cyclical recovery is the dominant force. The current price of A$1.17 sits 17% below N1's implied A$1.41, 70% above N2's implied A$0.69, and 43% below N4's A$2.05. It prices a world where the cyclical recovery thesis carries dominant weight and the structural risks identified across the specialist assessments carry residual weight.

executiveSummary.lede:
Resimac just delivered its best half-year since FY22 -- EBIT up 163%, operating cash flow
swinging A$456 million positive -- and the market re-rated the stock from below book value to
a 17% premium in five trading days. Organic home loan growth was 4% in a sector growing at
25%. Management attributes the margin recovery to RBA rate cuts, not operational
improvement. The question is whether the earnings inflection marks the start of a recovery or
the peak of a temporary reprieve.
executiveSummary.whatHappened:
For two years through mid-2024, Resimac traded at 68–77 cents per dollar of book value -- a
market verdict that the franchise would not earn its cost of capital. Net income had halved from
its FY22 peak, the home loan book was contracting, and the CEO resigned in July 2024. Within
eighteen months: new CEO appointed, A$1.4 billion Westpac auto portfolio acquired, New
Zealand exited, A$161 million returned to shareholders at nearly five times annual earnings,
ASIC civil penalty proceedings commenced -- and no finalised strategy disclosed. Then three
RBA rate cuts totalling 75 basis points widened net interest margin by 15 basis points, and the
1H26 results drove the share price from A$0.94 to A$1.17 on five times average volume.
executiveSummary.whatTheEvidenceSays:
The evidence-weighted assessment is approximately A$1.02, roughly 13% below the current
price. The gap reflects a disagreement about what is driving the company. The market is pricing
a lender passing through a cyclical trough whose earnings will continue recovering as credit
conditions normalise. The evidence gives greater weight to a different reading: that the recovery
is externally driven by rate cuts, and that underneath it the company is losing competitive
ground -- growing at one-sixth the sector rate, with no quantified strategy, governed by a
63.56% controlling shareholder who chairs the Audit Committee. The gap is directional but not
definitive. Under plausible shifts in a single variable, it narrows to 2–4%.
executiveSummary.whatToWatch:
The FY26 annual result, due August 2026, is the event that determines which reading is correct.
Two numbers matter.
--- PAGE 2 ---
Organic home loan AUM growth, measured against PEXA sector data. Above 8%: the
competitive gap is closing, the evidence-weighted assessment rises toward the current price,
and the stock likely tests the A$1.23–1.24 zone where prior rallies stalled. Below 5%: the
company is still losing share, the assessment falls toward A$0.88–0.92, and the two-year period
of below-book pricing re-enters the frame.
Group net interest margin with the RBA on hold at 3.60%. Above 150 basis points: the franchise
retains margin without rate-cut support. Below 145 basis points: the margin recovery was
borrowed from the rate cycle and is already in the price.
Before August, the strategy disclosure promised for early 2026 will either provide quantified
targets that make a turnaround credible or deliver another round of aspirational language -- and
that distinction reshapes what is possible from here.

content.text: Australian non-bank lending is a wholesale-funded credit origination sector whose structural niche is widening -- regulatory constraints on banks are progressively redirecting prime borrowers toward non-bank lenders, expanding the addressable market even as competition for that market intensifies.
Market Size & Trajectory

The broader non-depository financing industry generated approximately A$40.5 billion in revenue in 2025, compounding at 13.5% annually over the preceding five years. The securitisation market that funds it reached record dimensions in 2024: A$80 billion in public issuance across 100 transactions, up 43% on 2023. Non-bank RMBS specifically hit A$33.9 billion, with non-conforming issuance at A$16.7 billion. Metric 2022 2023 2024 2025E Non- ~31 ~34 ~37 ~40.5 depository financing revenue (A$bn) Total public ~50 52.3 80.0 62–75 securitisation --- PAGE 3 --- issuance (A$bn) Non-bank ~25 ~25 33.9 ~34+ RMBS issuance (A$bn) Non-ADI ~8% ~8% ~10% ~11% share of new origination flow Non-bank lenders hold approximately 4% of outstanding mortgage credit but capture a disproportionate share of new origination -- PEXA reported non-ADI new loan volumes up 25.3% in FY25 versus 3.9% for major banks. Growth is accelerating at the flow level even as the stock share remains modest.

rows: Non-ADI share of new origination flow

content.text: The Participants

Four major banks control over 75% of Australia's A$2.2 trillion mortgage market, with CBA at 26% and Westpac at 21%. Their deposit franchises deliver a structural cost-of-funds advantage that no wholesale-funded lender can replicate on equivalent products. Within the non-bank segment, four scaled operators dominate: Liberty Financial (A$1.46 billion revenue, ~A$14 billion loan book), Pepper Money (A$21.8 billion AUM, A$10.3 billion in FY25 originations, subject to an acquisition proposal from Challenger), Firstmac (private, top-three by revenue, aggressive prime pricing), and Resimac (A$104 million revenue, repositioning after exiting New Zealand and losing its long-tenured CEO). Below them, approximately 30 non-bank RMBS issuers now operate -- a threefold increase over a decade -- though concentration among the top four in non-mortgage ABS has fallen from near-100% to approximately 50%.
The Basis of Competition

Competition operates asymmetrically across two fronts. On the borrower side, approximately 97% of non-bank origination flows through the broker channel, where switching costs are negligible. Brokers route each transaction to whichever lender's policy, turnaround speed, and pricing best fits the borrower's circumstances. No non-bank lender can lock in broker flow. The partial exception is white-label lending -- once embedded as a white-label provider, operational --- PAGE 4 --- switching costs create modest stickiness. Pepper's white-label program, contributing 52% of new mortgage originations, is the largest in the sector. On the funding side, the competitive game shifts to securitisation track record, pool credit quality, and issuer reputation. A long history of consistent issuance with contained losses compresses funding spreads and broadens investor access. New entrants pay wider spreads until they establish performance history. Scale in origination feeds a supply-side advantage: larger pools support more frequent and larger transactions, attracting broader investor participation, which lowers funding costs, which supports more competitive lending rates.
Where Profit Concentrates

Margin concentrates in non-conforming and specialist lending -- rates of 7% to 9%+ versus approximately 5.5% to 6.5% for prime -- reflecting both elevated credit risk and the absence of bank competition. A major bank prime variable mortgage was priced around 5.5% in late 2025. Non-bank prime rates sit 20 to 100 basis points above this. The blended margin for any non- bank lender depends critically on its mix between prime and non-conforming origination, which is why Pepper's pivot toward prime -- 70% of new home loan settlements in H1 2025 -- carries direct margin implications.
Structural Forces

Three forces are reshaping the industry. First, each APRA macroprudential tightening on ADIs -- the 3% serviceability buffer, the DTI cap effective February 2026 limiting ADI lending at DTI ratios above six -- structurally redirects borrowers toward non-bank lenders exempt from these constraints. Second, the broker channel now originates more than half of all new mortgages, distributing volume to non-banks that compete on policy flexibility rather than price alone. Third, the deepening of the securitisation investor base, with approximately half of 2024 RMBS orders from offshore accounts, has compressed funding spreads and expanded issuance capacity.
The Primary Industry Risk

Credit losses are the dominant risk. Prime mortgage arrears (30+ days) rose from 1.05% in December 2022 to 1.62% in December 2023 as cumulative mortgage repayment increases of 44% since May 2022 outpaced wage growth of 7%. Non-conforming arrears reached 3.98%. Three RBA rate cuts in 2025 have eased pressure -- the share of borrowers 90+ days behind fell to approximately 0.7% by late 2025, and negative equity remains below 1%. Arrears have not been tested through a full recession cycle in the post-GFC securitisation era; unemployment has not breached 5% during this period of non-bank expansion. --- PAGE 5 ---
Regulatory Architecture

The regulatory framework favours non-bank lenders by design. ADIs face APRA's prudential standards -- capital adequacy, liquidity coverage, macroprudential caps -- while non-ADI lenders operate under ASIC's responsible lending obligations without equivalent capital or leverage constraints. This asymmetry is the foundational competitive condition: every APRA tightening on banks expands the non-bank addressable market. APRA retains the power to extend macroprudential tools to non-ADI lenders but has not exercised it. The DTI cap, which applies only to ADIs, will progressively redirect prime borrowers with elevated leverage -- particularly property investors -- to non-bank lenders as rates fall and DTI ratios mechanically rise.
What the Analysis Could Not Determine

Three questions remain unresolved. First, the threshold at which non-bank market share triggers APRA's extension of macroprudential tools -- no public guidance exists on what "materially contributing to financial instability" means in practice. Second, whether the approximately A$20 billion AUM ceiling for a single issuer in structured credit markets is a binding constraint or can be expanded through alternative funding mechanisms. Third, the credit performance of the high-DTI prime borrowers now being redirected to non-bank lenders -- these borrowers are prime by credit quality but elevated by leverage, and their loss behaviour under stress has no historical analogue in non-bank portfolios. The structural force that matters most for interpreting the rest of this report is the regulatory asymmetry between ADIs and non-ADIs. It determines the size of the addressable market, the competitive intensity within it, and the latent risk that the advantage narrows. Every section that follows -- competitive positioning, financial performance, scenario construction -- depends on whether this asymmetry widens, holds, or contracts.

Resimac's competitive position is narrowing -- the company is the smallest scaled non-bank lender by revenue, growing organically below sector rates, and facing direct encroachment from larger peers with broader distribution and lower operating costs. The assessment rests on moderate confidence. The analyst identified 23 competitive strategy mentions across 14 documents, with 83% of competitive claims metric-supported. Risk disclosures are specific rather than boilerplate. The limiting factor is what management does not disclose: no market share data, no pricing comparisons, no broker concentration or retention --- PAGE 6 --- metrics, no post-acquisition integration performance. The company reports what it has done but not where it stands.
How the Company Competes

Resimac originates residential mortgages (prime and specialist) and asset finance, funded through securitisation, distributed almost entirely through the broker channel. Competition on the borrower side is transactional -- brokers select lenders per deal based on credit policy fit, turnaround speed, and pricing. Switching costs are effectively zero for brokers and weak for borrowers. No white-label program creates the modest stickiness that Pepper's 52%-of- originations program provides. On the funding side, competition turns on securitisation track record and pool credit quality, where established issuers earn compressed spreads relative to newer entrants. This two-front structure means Resimac must compete continuously for broker flow while maintaining investor confidence in its loan pools.
Sources of Competitive Advantage

The primary advantage is intangible: a 40-year securitisation track record with nearly A$50 billion in cumulative issuance since 1987. This history provides access to the full depth of the domestic and offshore RMBS investor base, including UK sterling tranches. Senior RMBS margins improved from 177 basis points in FY23 to 125 basis points in 1H26, with the December 2025 Prime RMBS pricing at +95 basis points. FY25 issuance totalled A$4.3 billion, with a further A$3.0 billion in 1H26. Aggregate funding limits exceed A$10 billion. This advantage is real but shared. Liberty, Pepper, and Firstmac all possess equivalent programmatic securitisation access and comparable issuance histories. The track record prevents competitive erosion from subscale entrants below but provides no differentiation against peers. No network effects, no cost advantages, and no efficient scale barriers operate in Resimac's favour.
Evidence of Direction

Metric Direction Detail Organic home loan Below sector 4% vs 25.3% non-ADI AUM growth new loan volume growth (PEXA FY25) Asset finance AUM Acquisition-driven 127% headline; ~80% growth from Westpac auto run-off portfolio --- PAGE 7 --- Active brokers Recovering 1,272 -> 1,897 (49% growth 2H23–1H24), continuing at 14–25% Operating leverage Negative Opex +21% vs NII +7% in FY25 Cost-to-income Deteriorating 48.8% (1H24) -> 53.6% (FY25) vs Liberty at 28% Impairment expense Accelerating A$7.9m -> A$22.6m (FY24–FY25, +184%) Stage 3 collective Rising +87% to A$200m in balances 1H25 Home loan NIM Compressed 132bps vs group NIM of 154bps; asset finance NIM fell 37bps post-acquisition The recovery from FY24's trough is real -- settlements up 14%, broker count expanding, 1H26 normalised ROE reaching 15.5%. But the recovery is predominantly acquisition-driven and supported by capital management rather than organic competitive gains.

content.text: Vulnerabilities

Pepper Money is the most direct threat. Pepper's FY25 originations of A$10.3 billion are approximately double Resimac's A$5.8 billion in settlements. Pepper's AUM of A$21.8 billion exceeds Resimac's A$15.9 billion by 37%. The Challenger acquisition proposal, if completed, would bring institutional capital backing that widens the gap further. The APRA DTI cap effective February 2026 will direct high-DTI prime borrowers to all non-bank lenders, intensifying competition for a newly available pool where scale and distribution breadth advantage Pepper and Liberty over Resimac. ASIC civil penalty proceedings for hardship compliance failures (January 2022 to February 2024) are before the courts. The financial penalty is one dimension; the reputational impact on RMBS investor confidence and the potential for court-imposed compliance conditions are the larger risks. Leadership continuity is a further vulnerability -- CEO resignation (July 2024), --- PAGE 8 --- interim period, new CEO appointment (April 2025), New Zealand exit, and board restructuring occurred within 18 months. The strategy is still being developed.
Gaps in the Evidence

No market share data or trend analysis. No broker retention or attrition metrics. No pricing power evidence. No post-acquisition integration performance for the Westpac auto portfolio against underwriting assumptions. No technology capability benchmarks. The competitive picture is operationally detailed but strategically incomplete. Two observables would change this assessment. Organic home loan AUM growth exceeding 10% annualised -- closing the gap with sector growth rates -- would signal competitive stabilisation. Continued organic growth below sector averages, combined with impairment expense exceeding A$25 million annually or Stage 3 balances growing above 20% per half, would confirm the position is eroding rather than merely narrow.

Shareholder accountability at Resimac is procedurally intact but substantively inert -- a single ownership bloc controls 63.56% of voting rights, and the same individual chairs the Audit Committee while maintaining A$18.2 million in personal mortgage loans from the company and directing tens of millions in short-term lending to his related entities.
Control & Power Structure

Duncan Saville, through Somers Limited and related entities, holds 254,586,353 shares -- 63.56% of issued capital. This bloc determines the outcome of every ordinary resolution unilaterally: director elections, the advisory remuneration vote, and any shareholder- requisitioned motion. A second strike on remuneration cannot occur without Saville voting against the remuneration report. Independent shareholders collectively hold approximately 36%, insufficient to pass or block any resolution. The board has five directors. Three have compromised independence: Saville (controlling shareholder), Warren McLeland (former Executive Chairman, 12.1 million shares), and Susan Hansen (served as Interim CEO from July 2024 to April 2025, returned to non-executive status May 2025). Two qualify as independent under ASX guidelines -- Wayne Spanner and Caroline Waldron -- though Spanner chairs the Board, Remuneration & Nomination Committee, and Risk & Compliance Committee simultaneously, concentrating oversight of pay design, risk appetite, and board governance in one person. The company acknowledges two ASX Corporate Governance Principles non-compliances: Board Chair also chairing Remuneration & Nomination --- PAGE 9 --- (Recommendation 2.1) and Risk Committee not chaired by an independent director (Recommendation 7.1). Executive turnover has been near-total within 18 months. CEO McWilliam resigned July 2024, Hansen served as interim CEO, Pete Lirantzis was appointed April 2025, CFO Azzopardi resigned January 2024, CIO Muhammad resigned November 2024, and leadership was refreshed across operations, credit, technology, product, and distribution during FY25. The CEO has been in role approximately 14 months and is still developing strategy for board review.
Alignment

Saville bears the full economic consequences of value destruction -- 63.56% of every dollar of lost market capitalisation falls on his bloc. This is a genuine alignment mechanism. As the overwhelmingly dominant equity holder, his financial incentives are structurally tied to share price performance in a way that dispersed ownership cannot replicate. The capital return program supports this reading: A$161 million in dividends and franking credits distributed across FY25 to 1H26, corporate debt fully repaid, and a buyback that is reducing share count rather than offsetting dilution. Approximately 63.56% of all distributions flow to Saville. The limit of this alignment is that it substitutes principal concentration for governance. Saville's interests and minority shareholders' interests are aligned only as long as both are served by the same actions. Related party transactions -- short-term loans to Saville entities, insurance procurement through a Saville-controlled insurer, asset disposal to a director-related company -- represent points where the interests could diverge. Each transaction is characterised as arm's-length. The cumulative volume and variety create structural conflict that governance controls are designed to prevent rather than manage.
Accountability Gaps

The most material gap is the absence of any return-on-capital metric in compensation. No ROE, ROIC, or ROA hurdle appears in either STI or LTI, despite ROE being disclosed as a company performance metric. AUM growth appears in both STI and LTI, creating a direct incentive to acquire assets regardless of return. The Westpac auto portfolio added A$1.1 billion to AUM -- supporting the 127% asset finance AUM growth headline -- while delivering A$4.5 million in operating profit and being described by management as a run-off portfolio. The CEO's FY25 STI was paid in full against normalised NPAT that declined from A$43.1 million to A$39.7 million, impairment expense up 184%, and a cost-to-income ratio that deteriorated. No specific KPI thresholds or outcomes versus targets are disclosed. The LTI includes subjective qualitative hurdles -- technology strategy, digitisation, data security -- that are --- PAGE 10 --- difficult to assess objectively and easy for a sympathetic board to deem achieved. The longest management must remain exposed to the share price is three years, with no post-vesting holding requirement, against a mortgage credit cycle of five to seven years.
Red Flags & Open Items
ASIC commenced civil penalty proceedings in May 2025 for hardship compliance failures under section 47 of the NCCP Act, covering January 2022 to February 202

Resimac reduced its home loan Loss Given Default assumption by 50 basis points during FY25 -- releasing approximately A$4 million in provisioning -- while impairment expense surged 184%, Stage 3 balances increased 87%, and write-offs nearly tripled. The directional conflict between a loosening provisioning assumption and tightening credit conditions is the central earnings quality finding. Deloitte issued an unqualified opinion with no emphasis of matter. Severity assessment: medium. --- PAGE 11 ---
Where the Discretion Sits

Expected credit loss provisioning is the dominant area of management judgment. The ECL balance of A$56.5 million at 30 June 2025 rests on probability-weighted macroeconomic scenarios (25% Upside, 50% Base, 25% Downside), Loss Given Default assumptions revised downward during FY25, staging classifications where management determines migration thresholds, and discretionary overlays. Small changes across these interacting assumptions compound into material differences. No sensitivity disclosure quantifies the ECL impact of shifting scenario weights or reversing the LGD revision -- this is a verified absence in the financial statements. The dollar magnitude is substantial. Reversing the 50 basis point LGD reduction across the A$13.4 billion home loan book would add approximately A$4 million to the ECL. Increasing the Downside scenario weighting from 25% to 50% could add a further A$8–15 million, based on the severity gap between the Downside assumptions (negative 1.2% GDP, 8.0% unemployment) and the Base case (2.3% GDP, 5.4% unemployment). Combined, a plausible alternative set of reasonable assumptions could produce an ECL balance A$15–25 million above the reported figure -- equivalent to 43–72% of FY25 statutory NPAT of A$34.6 million. The ECL model was rebuilt during FY25, changing the mechanical basis of the largest provision on the balance sheet. Home loan ECL coverage fell from 28 to 22 basis points during the year. The 1H26 results show early stabilisation: impairment expense declined to A$9.7 million from A$14.8 million in 1H25, and collective provision balances declined 5%. Whether the LGD revision proves correct depends on realised loss experience over the next 12–18 months.
What the Auditor Tells You

Deloitte identified two Key Audit Matters: ECL provisioning and goodwill impairment. The ECL KAM specifically referenced the model rebuild, signalling auditor concern about new model implementation risk beyond routine provisioning judgment. The goodwill KAM reflects market capitalisation trading below book value at two consecutive balance dates -- an explicit impairment indicator under AASB 136 that triggers mandatory assessment. Deloitte applied enhanced procedures to both and reached an unqualified conclusion. Non-audit fees rose to 18.5% of total Deloitte fees in FY24, driven by technology consulting, below the 30% concern threshold but trending upward. --- PAGE 12 ---
Balance Sheet Fragilities

Goodwill of A$28.4 million is allocated to two CGUs: Home Loan Lending Business (A$21.7 million) and Resimac Asset Finance (A$6.7 million). Under a stress scenario at a 15.6% discount rate, HLLB headroom turns negative by A$10 million. Base-case headroom is not disclosed, preventing the reader from assessing how much additional stress triggers impairment. The A$21.7 million represents 5.9% of equity. The ASIC civil penalty proceedings carry no recognised contingent liability. No quantum, no range of outcomes, and no probability assessment are disclosed. ASIC penalties under the NCCP Act can reach the greater of A$11.1 million, three times the benefit obtained, or 10% of annual turnover. Against Resimac's A$170.5 million net interest income, a 10% turnover penalty would be A$17.1 million. Combined with the Financial Contribution Program for customer refunds, total exposure could plausibly reach A$20–30 million under an adverse scenario -- 58– 87% of one year's statutory NPAT. Asset finance impairment of A$25.4 million in FY25 represented 112% of total group impairment despite asset finance being 16% of loan assets. The Westpac auto portfolio entered the balance sheet at fair value with zero ECL at initial recognition; provisioning costs will build as the portfolio seasons.
What to Watch in the Next Accounts

Track the ratio of actual write-offs to impairment expense. Write-offs ran at A$3.4 million, A$7.4 million, and A$21.0 million across FY23–FY25, while impairment expense ran at A$2.2 million, A$11.6 million, and A$22.6 million. If write-offs equal or exceed the impairment charge, the provision build is insufficient and future earnings are overstated. Watch for any reversal of the LGD reduction, which would signal the FY25 assumption was premature. Watch for the first disclosure of ECL coverage on the Westpac auto portfolio specifically, and for any change in scenario weights -- a shift toward heavier Downside weighting would indicate management's macro outlook has deteriorated. The ASIC proceedings timeline will determine when a reliable estimate becomes required under AASB 137. The single largest accounting fragility is ECL provisioning. Under alternative reasonable assumptions -- reversed LGD, heavier Downside weighting, aligned staging -- the ECL balance could be A$15–25 million above reported levels, reducing statutory NPAT by 43–72%.

Resimac entered FY26 without a finalised strategy, and 72% of its forward guidance is directional or vague -- the company has one testable financial commitment in its entire --- PAGE 13 --- guidance portfolio.
What Has Been Committed

Metric Guided Target Type Period Westpac auto A$12.0m Hard FY26 portfolio commitment operating profit (quantified, public) Westpac Decreasing as Directional FY26 portfolio AUM customers repay NZ lending Run-off (no new Mechanical Ongoing segment origination) FY26 strategy Five-point Aspiration (no FY26 qualitative plan; metrics) detail "early next year" The A$12 million Westpac contribution is the sole quantified forward commitment. On a A$1.418 billion acquired portfolio, it implies a pre-tax return on assets of approximately 0.85%. As the portfolio runs off, the target must be achieved on a declining asset base. No cost-to-income target, no ROE target, no organic AUM growth rate, and no margin guidance have been provided for FY26.

content.text: Resimac earns a net interest margin of 1.5–1.6% on a A$16 billion securitisation-funded loan book, operates at a cost-to-income ratio above 50%, and converts that margin into mid-single- digit returns on equity in a trough year -- but the 1H26 half-year shows annualised EBIT nearly double FY25, and the question is whether the improvement reflects a genuine credit cycle turn or a temporary reprieve driven entirely by RBA rate cuts.
Financial Trajectory

FY22 and prior are not directly comparable with FY23 onward. ² 1H26 reported net income A$20.9m, annualised. The arc divides into three phases. FY22 was the peak -- EBIT margin of 26.3% and ROE of 18.4% on the pre-reclassification revenue base. FY23 through FY25 was sustained compression: EBIT fell from A$120 million to A$44 million as impairment expense rose tenfold (A$2.2 million to A$22.6 million), SG&A grew faster than revenue, and the home loan book contracted A$1.8 billion before partially recovering. The chairman described FY24 as the toughest year for non-banks since the GFC. 1H26 is the inflection: EBIT margin expanded 460 --- PAGE 17 --- basis points same-half year-on-year, impairment halved, and operating cash flow swung from negative A$251 million to positive A$205 million. The FY25 ROE of 9.5% is almost entirely leverage-driven. EBIT margin contracted and asset turnover fell; the equity multiplier expanded from 36x to 46x because A$1.9 billion in securitisation debt was added for the Westpac acquisition while equity declined A$51 million through dividends exceeding earnings. The 1H26 normalised ROE of 15.5% is partly a denominator effect -- equity fell from A$416 million (FY24) to A$365 million (FY25) through A$161 million in capital returns. On a stable FY24 equity base, the ROE improvement would be materially smaller.
Cash Conversion

Earnings are cash-backed relative to the asset base. Accruals are immaterial against A$16.9 billion in total assets, and no forensic accounting flags were triggered. The deeply negative operating cash flow in FY24 and FY25 (negative A$109 million and negative A$431 million) is mechanical, not diagnostic: new loan origination is an operating cash outflow for a lender, and the Westpac acquisition added A$1.4 billion to the book. FY23 generated A$2.0 billion of operating cash flow precisely because the loan book was contracting. The 1H26 swing to positive A$205 million confirms the business generates cash when book growth moderates. The qualifier from the accounting section is separate: the FY25 LGD revision released approximately A$4 million in provisioning during a period of rising credit stress, meaning reported earnings sit at the favourable end of a plausible range that extends 43–72% below the reported figure under alternative reasonable ECL assumptions.
Cost Structure

For a securitisation-funded lender, the cost structure decomposes into three layers: funding cost (securitisation interest expense, the largest line, reported within cost of revenue), credit cost (impairment expense, the swing variable), and operating cost (SG&A). Funding cost is determined by RMBS market conditions and Resimac's issuer spreads. Senior RMBS margins improved from 177 basis points in FY23 to 125 basis points in 1H26 -- a tailwind that flows directly to gross margin. Credit cost is where profitability is won or lost. Impairment expense moved from A$2.2 million (FY23) to A$22.6 million (FY25), a swing of A$20.4 million that exceeded the entire EBIT decline of A$35 million over the same period. At 1H26, impairment of A$9.7 million (A$19.4 million annualised) accounts for the majority of the EBIT margin recovery. The concentration of credit losses is pronounced: asset finance generated A$25.4 million of the A$22.6 million total group impairment in FY25 -- 112% of the group figure --- PAGE 18 --- -- despite representing 16% of loan assets. This segment was built primarily through acquisition during a period of record insolvencies. SG&A/revenue deteriorated from 7.7% (FY24) to 8.7% (FY25) before compressing to 5.4% in 1H26. The 1H26 improvement implies annualised SG&A of A$56.6 million, down A$34.8 million from FY25. The gap is too large to be seasonal. FY25 SG&A included A$1.25 million in executive termination benefits and ASIC-related professional fees; the remainder of the reduction likely reflects cost actions under the new CEO. A ±10% revenue move on the current cost base produces a modest EBIT impact because the gross-to-EBIT conversion is thin -- FY25 gross profit of A$165 million yielded only A$44 million of EBIT. The leverage is in impairment: a return to FY25 impairment levels (A$22.6 million) on flat revenue compresses EBIT margin from the 1H26 level of 7.6% back toward 4%.
The Inflection

Metric 1H25 1H26 Change Revenue (A$m) 511 523 +2.4% EBIT (A$m) 15.2 39.9 +163% EBIT margin 3.0% 7.6% +460bps Impairment 14.8 9.7 -34% (A$m) OCF (A$m) (251) 205 +A$456m swing Group NIM (bps) 146 161 +15bps D/E 44.67 40.47 Deleveraging Three converging factors drive the half-year improvement: NIM widened 15 basis points to 161 basis points as three RBA rate cuts (75 basis points cumulative) flowed through the variable-rate book; impairment halved as credit stress moderated and the FY25 write-off policy acceleration cleared stale receivables; and SG&A compressed. Revenue grew 2.4%. The entire profitability recovery is cost-side and margin-side, with no contribution from top-line growth. If the rate cycle pauses, impairment re-accelerates, or SG&A reverts, the improvement reverses on a flat revenue base. --- PAGE 19 ---
Balance Sheet Position

The balance sheet must be read through the securitisation lens. The D/E ratio of 40x and D/A of 96.7% reflect approximately A$16 billion of limited-recourse securitisation funding matched to underlying loan assets -- not corporate leverage. Corporate recourse debt was fully repaid in 1H26. Cash stood at A$778 million. The relevant measure is the equity-to-assets ratio: 2.4% at 1H26 (A$398 million on A$16.65 billion). This is the capital buffer absorbing credit losses before securitisation investors are affected. The buffer is thin. The A$25.4 million asset finance impairment in FY25 alone consumed 6.4% of equity. If the accounting section's stress scenario materialised concurrently with the ASIC contingency (A$15–19 million incremental ECL plus A$20–30 million ASIC exposure), the combined A$35–49 million impact would reduce equity by 8.8–12.3%. The balance sheet could absorb this without triggering solvency risk, but the funding model depends on investor confidence -- and a step-change reduction in equity would be visible to every RMBS investor evaluating Resimac's next transaction.
Where the Financial Evidence Meets the Other Sections

The financials confirm the competitive section's narrowing assessment: organic home loan AUM growth of 4% in FY25 against 25.3% sector volume growth is a market share loss visible in the revenue deceleration. The governance section's finding -- no return-on-capital metric in compensation -- is amplified by the DuPont decomposition showing ROE driven entirely by the equity multiplier rather than operating performance. The accounting section's ECL fragility and the financial section's impairment sensitivity share the same trigger: a credit cycle deterioration that compounds across provisioning, earnings, and equity simultaneously. The single financial metric to watch at the next result is annualised impairment expense. If FY26 impairment stays below A$20 million, the 1H26 inflection is confirmed and EBIT margin above 6% is sustainable. If it exceeds A$22 million -- FY25 levels -- the recovery was cyclical noise, and the profitability trajectory reverts to the trough.

The market has re-rated Resimac from below book value to a 17% premium in six months, pricing a cyclical recovery that five of six specialist assessments in this report qualify or contradict. --- PAGE 20 ---
How We Got Here

rows: Resimac has passed through a credit cycle trough. The 1H26 inflection is the first instalment of a multi- period recovery as rate cuts widen NIM, impairment moderates, and the securitisation franchise generates returns above cost of equity when the cycle cooperates. The franchise is durable; the cycle was the problem.

The competitive position is eroding – organic growth lags the sector sixfold – but the erosion is temporarily masked by rate-driven NIM expansion and cyclical impairment relief. When the macro tailwind fades, the structural narrowing reasserts in compressed margins and continued share loss.

The controlling shareholder structure is the organising principle. A$161 million in distributions at 4.7 times earnings, extensive related party transactions, and no return-on-capital incentive in compensation describe a company managed to maximise distributions from favourable conditions rather than build a durable franchise.

content.text: From mid-2023 through mid-2024, the market valued Resimac's equity at 68–77 cents per dollar of book value. This is the pricing of a lender whose return on equity will remain below its cost of equity on a sustained basis. The verdict was not unreasonable: net income had halved from FY22 to FY24, NIM was compressing under competitive pressure from major bank repricing, the home loan book was contracting, and the cost-to-income ratio was deteriorating. The share price fell from A$1.24 in February 2024 to A$0.79 by July 2024 -- a 36% decline over 108 days -- after the FY24 results delivered a 48% NPAT decline, a CEO resignation, and a New Zealand exit. The market's day-one reaction to that result was negative 5.6%, the only materially negative announcement response in the three-year observation window. The belief began shifting in February 2025, and the catalyst was forward-looking rather than backward-looking. The 1H25 results were poor -- NPAT fell a further 33.5%, impairment surged 517% -- yet the market rallied 8.7% on the day. The decomposition reveals why: enterprise value expanded while earnings compressed. The market was paying more for less current profit, which is the signature of a forward-looking belief upgrade. The most likely catalyst was the Westpac auto portfolio acquisition, which carried the first quantified forward commitment management had made in the entire observation period -- A$6 million in 2H25 operating profit and A$12 million in FY26. From that point, the share price advanced 55.7% over 318 days to A$1.23 by October 2025, a gradual and orderly accumulation with balanced volume rather than momentum-driven buying. The October 2025 to February 2026 period was a shallow pullback -- 5.3% over 97 days on unremarkable volume -- consistent with the market digesting the re-rating rather than reversing it. Then the 1H26 results arrived on 23 February 2026 and produced a discontinuity: the share price advanced from A$0.94 to A$1.17 in five trading days, with the 25 February session showing five times average volume on an 18.9% single-day advance. EBIT had expanded 163% same-half year-on-year. Operating cash flow swung A$456 million from negative to positive. Impairment halved. The market responded by pushing the price-to-book ratio to its second- highest reading in three years.
Where the Market Sits Now

At A$1.17, the market capitalisation of approximately A$463 million represents 1.17 times the 1H26 book equity of A$398 million. Six months earlier, the same equity traded at 0.88 times book. A price-to-book above 1.0 implies the market expects return on equity to exceed cost of equity on a sustained basis. The 1H26 normalised ROE of 15.5% supports that expectation on its face -- but the financial section established that this ROE is flattered by an equity base reduced --- PAGE 21 --- through A$161 million in capital returns and driven by NIM widening from RBA rate cuts rather than operational improvement. Revenue grew 2.4%. The entire profitability recovery is externally sourced. The divergence between the market's pricing and the specialist evidence is pronounced. The competitive section assesses the position as narrowing, with organic growth lagging the sector. The governance section identifies structural conflicts that are stable and unremedied. The accounting section finds the ECL estimate at the favourable end of a range that extends 43– 72% below reported NPAT. The guidance section rates credibility as low, with no quantified forward targets beyond a single acquisition contribution. The financial section finds the 1H26 inflection entirely dependent on rate cuts and credit cycle moderation. The market is pricing a cyclical recovery; the evidence base describes a structurally challenged franchise receiving a macro tailwind. It is possible the market is correct and the specialists are overweighting structural concerns relative to cyclical momentum. RBA rate cuts are real. The credit cycle moderation is observable in the data. The capital return program has rewarded shareholders. A four-day post-result window is too brief to determine whether the re-rating is durable or reactive. What Would Change It A sustained move above the A$1.23–1.24 zone -- the level at which prior rallies in February 2024 and October 2025 both stalled -- would require confirmation that the 1H26 trajectory extends into the full FY26 year: impairment below A$20 million annualised, NIM above 155 basis points, and evidence of organic volume recovery. A reversion toward the 0.70–0.80 times book range that prevailed for two years would follow from impairment re-accelerating in 2H26, NIM compressing as the rate cycle pauses, or the ASIC contingency crystallising at a quantum that impairs the equity base. The question the belief trajectory raises and cannot yet answer: is the market pricing a multi- period earnings recovery that will be confirmed by FY26 and FY27 results, or has it captured a four-day reaction to a single half-year result whose drivers -- rate cuts and credit cycle moderation -- are external, temporary, and already reflected in the price?
